title: Shubnikov-de Haas Characterization of Superconductor-Semiconductor Heterostructures
authors: A. M. Zimmerman, Saeed Fallahi, Sergei Gronin, Tyler Lindemann, Patrick Sohr, Ray Kallaher, Alejandro Alcaraz Ramirez, Georg W. Winkler, Samuel M. L. Teicher, William Cole, Sebastian Heedt, Eoin O'Farrell, Gijs de Lange, Roman Lutchyn, Michael J. Manfra, John Watson
abstract: Hybrid superconductor-semiconductor nanostructures are a central component for research spanning condensed matter physics and quantum information processing. Continued progress relies critically on the ability to characterize, control, and optimize several intrinsic material properties including spin-orbit coupling, band offsets, and disorder in a device-relevant stack that necessarily couples the electronic states of a superconducting metal film and a semiconductor. Here we report a new method to extract fundamental material parameters utilizing simple Shubnikov-de Haas (SdH) oscillation measurements in heterostructures in which metallic electronic states are coupled to a two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) residing in an InAs quantum well beneath an aluminum thin film. Proper analysis of the full magnetoresistance data facilitates extraction of the quantum well carrier density, spin-orbit coupling strength, and both transport and quantum scattering times. Most importantly, the extracted scattering times in the 2DEG are impacted by the metal-semiconductor coupling strength allowing us to quickly gain information on proximity-induced superconducting gap without any fabrication or mK measurements. The wealth of information that is accessed with these simple measurements positions this methodology as an important tool for hybrid materials optimization.
summary: This paper presents a new way to quickly characterize the material properties of superconductor-semiconductor heterostructures using simple magnetoresistance measurements. By analyzing SdH oscillations, researchers can extract spin-orbit coupling and scattering times, even estimating the superconducting gap without needing ultra-low temperatures. This provides a much faster feedback loop for optimizing materials used in topological quantum computing.
main_problem: The need for a rapid, non-destructive method to characterize essential material properties (spin-orbit coupling, carrier density, and scattering times) in hybrid superconductor-semiconductor heterostructures without complex nanofabrication or mK measurements.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that Shubnikov-de Haas (SdH) oscillations can extract fundamental parameters and even estimate the proximity-induced superconducting gap by analyzing changes in scattering lifetimes.
method: Analysis of SdH oscillations in unpatterned 2D films using high-pass filtering, FFT for subband density, and two-band conductivity model fitting.
model_or_system: A hybrid nanostructure consisting of an Aluminum (Al) thin film coupled to a two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) in an InAs or InAsSb quantum well.
key_observables: Carrier density (n), Rashba spin-orbit coupling strength (alpha), transport scattering time (tau_t), quantum scattering time (tau_q), and the ratio tau_t/tau_q.
important_parameters: Metal-semiconductor coupling strength (Gamma), proximity-induced superconducting gap (Delta_ind), and scattering regimes (long-range vs. short-range).
main_assumptions: The system can be modeled as two parallel coupled electronic bands where the metal has extremely high density and low resistivity.
figures_summary: Figure 3 shows extracted parameters (SOC, density, lifetimes, and ratio) vs. Sb fraction in InAsSb; Figure 4 plots the induced gap against the transport lifetime.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the characterization problem, describes the hybrid S-Sm system and the SdH measurement methodology, validates the method using InAsSb alloying, and demonstrates the predictive power for the induced superconducting gap.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Raw magnetoresistance R(B) showing SdH oscillations on a magnetoresistance background from the combined semi- conductor metal system. Data is from a sample presented in Fig. 4. Inset: schematic of the heterostructure, Al/top barrier (TB)/QW/back barrier (BB), with cyclotron orbits in the QW. Arrows indicate disorder scattering (τ) and escape into the metal (Γ).

FIG. 2. SdH analysis steps for the data shown in Fig. 1. (a) Background-subtracted oscillations ∆R vs. 1/B showing beating from spin-orbit coupling. (b) Inset of panel c: FFT of ∆R(1/B) for B < 1 T, showing two spin-split peaks and Lorentzian fit (green). (c) Raw R(B) with two-channel magnetoresistance fit (red dashed). The fit is performed over the entire data range (d) ∆R vs. 1/B with full SdH model fit (red, dashed) used to extract τq. Fit is performed for B < 0.7 T.

FIG. 3. SdH-extracted parameters vs. Sb fraction in the QW for InAs1−xSbx quantum wells. (a) Rashba SOC α. (b) Car- rier density n. (c) Transport (τt, circles) and quantum (τq, squares) scattering times. (d) Ratio τt/τq.

FIG. 4. Induced superconducting gap ∆ind vs. transport scat- tering time τt. Data points (circles) are independent QPC and SdH measurements on the same stacks. Curves show the model [Eqs. (1)–(6)] for τt semi = ∞(clean limit), 1.5, and 2.5 ps with ∆0 = 305 µeV.
